ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Beijing-style bean paste (老北京豆酱). The closest parent term for fermented soybean paste food product (FOODON_03311737) is used as the nearest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC for soybean paste (fermented) is the closest defensible parent. No Beijing-specific variant code exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Beijing bean paste or a sufficiently close fermented bean paste condiment. Assigned none.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Traditional Beijing Bean Paste is approximately one tablespoon (18 g), used as a condiment, dipping sauce, or flavouring base in noodle dishes and stir-fries.

nutrient_source.title: Soybean paste, fermented (黄豆酱 / 豆酱) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods; cross-referenced with Chinese fermented soybean paste compositional data from FAO/INFOODS East Asian food composition tables
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central; FAO/INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA entry for 老北京豆酱 (Old Beijing Bean Paste) exists. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entry for fermented soybean paste (NDB No. 16113, 'Miso' and related soybean paste entries) and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS compositional data for Chinese-style fermented bean paste. Beijing-style doujiang is a wheat-and-soybean fermented paste with broadly similar macronutrient profile to yellow soybean paste (黄豆酱). Energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, and fibre values are adapted accordingly. Sodium is notably high due to salt used in fermentation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of fermented soybean-based condiments — analogous estimate based on soybean paste / miso GI data; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for 老北京豆酱 (Traditional Beijing Bean Paste). GI is estimated at 35 (low) based on the GI of fermented soybean paste and miso (GI approximately 30–40, low category) as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. The high protein, fibre, and fermentation-derived organic acids in soybean-based pastes are known to attenuate glycaemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 5.44) / 100 ≈ 1.9 (serving = 18 g, carbohydrate = 30.2 × 0.18 = 5.44 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (~3520 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly limit consumption of Traditional Beijing Bean Paste due to its very high sodium content (approximately 3520 mg per 100 g); even a single tablespoon serving (18 g) contributes approximately 634 mg sodium, representing over 27% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate content with low glycemic index; high sodium secondary concern
type2_diabetes.guidance: Traditional Beijing Bean Paste has a low estimated glycemic index (~35) and a low glycemic load per serving (~1.9), making it acceptable in small quantities for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, portion control is essential given its moderate carbohydrate content (30.2 g per 100 g) and very high sodium, which may exacerbate cardiovascular comorbidities common in diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high sodium and moderate fat content; low saturated fat partially mitigating
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should use Traditional Beijing Bean Paste sparingly due to its very high sodium content (~3520 mg per 100 g), which is a major risk factor for hypertension and adverse cardiovascular outcomes; the relatively low saturated fat content (0.9 g per 100 g) and presence of polyunsaturated fats (3.1 g per 100 g) are partially mitigating factors, but sodium remains the primary concern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Traditional Beijing Bean Paste (老北京豆酱, Lǎo Běijīng Dòujiàng) is a classic Northern Chinese fermented condiment originating from Beijing. It is made primarily from soybeans and wheat flour that are fermented with salt and water over an extended period, producing a thick, deeply savoury paste with a complex umami flavour profile. The paste is characteristically dark brown to reddish-brown in colour, with a dense, slightly chunky texture that includes whole or partially broken fermented beans suspended in a rich sauce. The flavour is intensely salty, earthy, and umami-forward, with subtle sweetness from the fermented wheat component and a mild fermented aroma. In Beijing cuisine, this paste is most famously used as the primary sauce for Zhajiangmian (炸酱面), the iconic Beijing-style noodles with minced meat and vegetables. It is also used as a dipping sauce, a marinade base, and a flavouring agent in stir-fried dishes and braised meats. Nutritionally, Traditional Beijing Bean Paste provides moderate energy (approximately 218 kcal per 100 g), meaningful protein (10.5 g per 100 g) from the soybean base, and dietary fibre (4.2 g per 100 g). Its most clinically significant characteristic is its very high sodium content (approximately 3520 mg per 100 g), which makes it a high-concern condiment for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated to be low (approximately 35), consistent with other fermented soybean-based condiments, making it less problematic for glycaemic control when used in typical condiment quantities. The fermentation process contributes bioactive compounds and organic acids that may have modest probiotic and antioxidant properties, though these are not yet well-quantified for this specific product. As served in Singapore such as See Ya Tonight (今晚有局), this paste is typically presented as a component of a larger dish rather than consumed in isolation.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in bean paste?
Is Beijing bean paste the same as miso?
What dishes use Beijing bean paste?
Is Beijing bean paste gluten free?
What is the GI of fermented bean paste?

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for 老北京豆酱 (Traditional Beijing Bean Paste) was found in any primary approved source. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central fermented soybean paste data and FAO/INFOODS East Asian food composition references. Beijing-style doujiang is compositionally similar to yellow soybean paste (黄豆酱) but may vary by producer and recipe. GI is an analogous estimate based on fermented soybean paste GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central fermented soybean paste entries and FAO/INFOODS East Asian food composition data; no exact Beijing-style doujiang entry was found in any approved primary source. 2. Sodium value (~3520 mg per 100 g) is consistent with typical Chinese fermented bean pastes and is the primary clinical concern. 3. GI is an analogous estimate (GI ~35, low) based on fermented soybean paste and miso GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero for plant-based fermented paste with no hydrogenated fat ingredients. 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a plant-derived product containing no animal-derived cholesterol. 6. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; fermented soybean paste does not contain meaningful vitamin C. 7. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data for this product category. 8. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data. 9. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported; plant-based fermented paste is not a reliable source of B12. 10. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data. 11. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data. 12. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data. 13. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data. 14. The food is served at See Ya Tonight (今晚有局) in Singapore at SGD 17.94 per portion; this price reflects a restaurant dish rather than a condiment-only serving. 15. Halal status is marked false as traditional fermented bean paste may involve fermentation processes not certified halal; verification would be required for a specific product. 16. Aliases consistency verified: all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in the flat aliases list.
